evidence: Runtime seams are modeled as very large callback bags across multiple modules (for example `startupInitRuntime` + `StartupRuntime` + `startBackgroundServicesRuntime` in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`, `importerRuntime` in `goseg/importer/importer.go`, and many `Runtime*Ops` structs in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`).

Many defaults are thin pass-through assignments to package-level functions (for example `defaultImporterRuntime`, `defaultStartBackgroundServicesRuntime`, `newWorkflowRuntime`, and `runtime*Ops()` builders), which adds indirection layers without providing clear policy boundaries.

Dependency injection boundaries are inconsistent because default singletons/globals remain first-class paths (`DefaultBroadcastStateRuntime` in `goseg/broadcast/state.go`, default loop/runtime constructors in `goseg/broadcast/loop.go`, `DefaultEventRuntime` in `goseg/docker/events/events.go`), so abstraction contracts can be bypassed.

holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles reports large flat directories for all three involved packages: goseg/config/ has 41 files, goseg/shipworkflow/ has 44 files, and goseg/system/ has 39 files (all above the >10 mixed-concern threshold in the prompt guidance).

Within goseg/system, disk_fstab.go and disk_health.go are package-level facades that mostly translate and forward to groundseg/system/storage APIs, indicating import-affinity toward a subdomain while remaining in the broad parent package.

Within goseg/shipworkflow, lifecycle.go sits beside many distinct concern groups (newship_*, startram_*, urbit_operations_*, workflow_*), which indicates broad directory-level concern mixing rather than purpose-driven subfolders.

Within goseg/config, bootstrap.go (startup/init path + session key bootstrapping) and config.go (runtime context model + snapshot/read APIs) live in the same flat directory as many unrelated config domains (version/minio/netdata/wireguard), reducing navigability and ownership clarity.
holistic_context.structure in this packet provides directory_profiles but root_files and coupling_matrix are null, so objective boundary directionality signals are partially unavailable.

`goseg/config/config_update_schema.go` re-declares almost the same config surface as `goseg/structs/configs.go` (connectivity/runtime/startram/auth/penpai fields) through separate patch structs and apply plumbing, creating a second structural model of the same domain.

`goseg/system/disk/adapter.go` mirrors `goseg/system/storage` types/functions with mostly pass-through conversions (e.g., `FstabRecord`, parse/reconcile/read/write wrappers), adding an extra boundary without clear policy or behavior.

`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` and `goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint.go` both use large function-field runtime structs with many optional callbacks and normalization paths; tests like `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator_test.go` and `goseg/main_test.go` then need broad callback wiring maps/options to exercise behavior.

Protocol-conformance checks are spread across multiple test modules (`goseg/protocol/actions/actions_test.go`, `goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts_test.go`, `goseg/system/wifi_c2c_flow_test.go`, `goseg/uploadsvc/service_test.go`) with repeated contract/action alignment assertions rather than a shared conformance harness.

Config APIs mix full and abbreviated vocabulary for the same concept: `Conf()`, `StartConfEventLoop`, and `ConfChannel` coexist with `StartConfigEventLoop` and `configEventRuntime` in `goseg/config/bootstrap.go` and `goseg/config/config_events.go`.

Protocol action identifiers use inconsistent word ordering and token shape across related layers: `UploadActionOpenEndpoint`/`UploadActionReset` vs `C2CConnectAction` in `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go`, and `ActionUploadOpenEndpoint`/`ActionC2CConnect` in `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go`.

Runtime-injection suffixes are inconsistently stacked, producing low-signal distinctions such as `LoadStartramRegionsWithRuntime` vs `LoadStartramRegionsWithRuntimeState` and alias pairs like `StartConfEventLoopWithRuntime` in `goseg/broadcast/state_lifecycle.go` and `goseg/config/config_events.go`.

findings.summary: Large callback-bag runtimes create high indirection with low leverage and unclear ownership boundaries.
evidence: `startBackgroundServicesRuntime` and related startup runtime structs declare many callback fields and rely on grouped reflection validation tags, making wiring correctness depend on string-tagged metadata rather than typed module contracts.
`importerProvisionRuntime` embeds orchestration runtime bags and additional callbacks, blending storage/provision/workflow concerns into one seam object.
`runtime_ops.go` defines many parallel `Runtime*Ops` structs that are mostly function pointers to package-level helpers, so call chains grow but domain invariants are still enforced in callers.
`newWorkflowRuntime()` assembles multiple runtime bags and default emitters, then many workflow helpers re-resolve that runtime before executing simple operations.
findings.suggestion: Replace broad callback bags with a small number of cohesive role interfaces per use-case (for example startup init host/storage, import provisioning, workflow transition publishing). Move policy into those implementations, keep seams narrow, and remove string-tag-driven callback grouping where typed interfaces can enforce requirements at compile time.

findings.summary: Modules mix injectable runtimes with implicit global defaults, weakening abstraction honesty and dependency direction.
evidence: `broadcast` exposes `DefaultBroadcastStateRuntime()` and many call paths still default to it (`ReloadUrbits`, `StartBroadcastLoop`, runtime constructors), while also supporting explicit runtime injection.

Several broadcast entrypoints use optional varargs runtime parameters (`bootstrapBroadcastState(runtime ...*broadcastStateRuntime)`, `LoadStartramRegionsWithRuntime(runtime ...*broadcastStateRuntime)`), creating dual dependency semantics.
`events` similarly provides both delegate-based broker injection and process-wide default singleton (`DefaultEventRuntime()`), allowing hidden ambient dependencies.
findings.suggestion: Choose one dependency model per subsystem: explicit runtime injection for orchestrated paths, with globals limited to top-level composition roots. Remove vararg runtime fallbacks and convert default singletons into constructor wiring in bootstrap code.

findings.summary: Config snapshot structs act as broad parameter bags, creating cross-subsystem coupling through a central config hub.
evidence: `config.go` defines multiple aggregate settings snapshots (`StartupSettings`, `ImportSettings`, `C2CSettings`, `RuntimeContext`) that combine unrelated concerns.
Startup and importer runtime resolution consume these aggregate snapshots and then extract a subset of fields, indicating parameter-bag usage rather than focused capability contracts.
`main.go` C2C runtime path pulls `C2CSettingsSnapshot()` and falls back to direct `config.RuntimeContextSnapshot()` reads, showing inconsistent boundary use.
`subsystem_health.go` and orchestration code repeatedly snapshot/clone config-derived data (`Check502SettingsSnapshotFn`, settings.Piers copy), reinforcing central mutable-config gravity.
findings.suggestion: Split config snapshots into narrow domain providers (for example `StartramRuntimeView`, `ConnectivityPolicyView`, `ImportSwapPolicy`) owned by each subsystem boundary. Pass only required views into runtime constructors and avoid mixing direct config reads with snapshot structs in the same flow.

findings.summary: Disk fstab/health logic is organized as root system-package facades while behavior is actually owned by the storage subdomain.
evidence: disk_fstab.go defines local fstabRecord and forwarding helpers (parseFstabLine, reconcileFstabLines, readFstabLines, writeFstabLines) that delegate to groundseg/system/storage functions.
disk_health.go exports SmartResultsSnapshot, SetSmartResults, ListHardDisks, IsDevMounted, SmartCheckAllDrives and each delegates directly to groundseg/system/storage with minimal added behavior.
This is an import-affinity mismatch pattern: implementation coupling is centered on storage, but file placement remains in the broad parent package.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) Create `goseg/system/storagefacade/` (or `goseg/system/disk/`) as the explicit compatibility boundary and move `disk_fstab.go` and `disk_health.go` there with `git mv goseg/system/disk_fstab.go goseg/system/storagefacade/disk_fstab.go` and `git mv goseg/system/disk_health.go goseg/system/storagefacade/disk_health.go`. (2) Keep root-level API compatibility by adding thin deprecated forwarders in `goseg/system` only where needed. (3) Update imports with `rg -n "groundseg/system" goseg | rg "ListHardDisks|SmartCheckAllDrives|parseFstabLine|readFstabLines"` and adjust callers to the new folder boundary. (4) Validate with `go test ./goseg/system/... ./goseg/system/storage/...` and `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: The config package is overloaded as a flat directory mixing bootstrap/runtime-state concerns with many domain-specific adapters.
evidence: directory_profiles shows `goseg/config/` has 41 files in one level.
bootstrap.go performs process initialization responsibilities (default validation, path setup, session key generation, file creation/load, persistence).
config.go defines runtime context structures/settings snapshots and global state access APIs, which is a separate concern from startup bootstrap behavior.
File list in the same directory also includes domain-specific modules (`minio.go`, `netdata.go`, `wireguard.go`, `version.go`), indicating mixed ownership boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) Introduce subfolders `goseg/config/bootstrap/`, `goseg/config/runtime/`, and `goseg/config/domains/` (for service-specific config modules). (2) Move `bootstrap.go`/`bootstrap_test.go` first, then move runtime-context/state APIs from `config.go` into `runtime/`, then fold service-specific files into `domains/` in small batches. (3) Keep `goseg/config` as a stable facade package during migration with explicit exported forwarding functions/types to avoid breakage. (4) Update imports incrementally via `rg -n "groundseg/config" goseg` and verify each batch with `go test ./goseg/config/...` then `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Ship workflow code is kept in a single large directory despite distinct lifecycle/newship/startram/urbit domains.
evidence: directory_profiles shows `goseg/shipworkflow/` has 44 files in one level.
The file set includes clearly separate concern families (`newship_*`, `startram_*`, `urbit_operations_*`, `workflow_*`, `lifecycle.go`) without folder-level segmentation.
lifecycle.go contains generic lifecycle helpers while lifecycle_test.go is only a placeholder smoke test, which suggests weaker local ownership boundaries in the current placement.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) Create purpose folders under shipworkflow such as `newship/`, `startram/`, `urbit/`, and `lifecycle/`; keep root package as orchestration API entrypoint. (2) Move lowest-coupled helpers first (`lifecycle.go` + tests), then move `newship_*`, then `startram_*`, then `urbit_operations_*` families. (3) Add transitional forwarding files at old package paths for one migration window if external imports depend on current locations. (4) Update imports with `rg -n "groundseg/shipworkflow" goseg` and validate via `go test ./goseg/shipworkflow/...` then `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Protocol action/contract identity is split across multiple sibling packages with no single ownership boundary.
evidence: `actionids.go` stores raw canonical tokens (`NamespaceUpload`, `UploadOpenEndpoint`, `C2CConnect`).
`protocol_contracts.go` re-exports those tokens as `ActionNamespace*`/`Action*` and constructs governance IDs in a separate package.
`fixtures_test.go` anchors conformance checks to `protocol/actions` constants, creating a third dependency surface for the same contract identity space.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create `goseg/protocol/spec/` as the single protocol identity package (namespaces, verbs, binding map, contract-ID helpers); (2) move constants from `goseg/protocol/actionids/actionids.go` into `goseg/protocol/spec/actions.go`; (3) update `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go` and `goseg/protocol/conformance/fixtures_test.go` imports to use `protocol/spec` only; (4) remove redundant alias constants after call sites are migrated; (5) validate with `cd goseg && go test ./protocol/spec ./protocol/contracts ./protocol/conformance ./protocol/actions` and then `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Contract conformance tests are organized outside the README’s declared backend governance gate, weakening navigable policy-to-package alignment.
evidence: `README.md` fast backend gate command includes `./protocol/contracts` but omits `./protocol/conformance`.
`fixtures_test.go` enforces canonical fixture coverage and unknown-action rejection, which are governance-level compatibility checks.
`family_specs_test.go` validates contract-family metadata and ID expectations, showing conformance and contracts are one policy domain but split in gate and package focus.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) move `goseg/protocol/conformance/` under `goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/` to co-locate governance checks with contract specs; (2) update package imports and test package declarations to new path; (3) update README backend fast-gate command to `go test ./protocol/contracts/...` so both contract and conformance checks are included by default; (4) validate with `cd goseg && go test ./protocol/contracts/...` and `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Config schema and patch logic duplicate the core config model in parallel structures, increasing drift risk and change cost.
evidence: `ConfPatch` embeds multiple section patches (`ConnectivityPatch`, `RuntimePatch`, `StartramPatch`, `PenpaiPatch`, `AuthSessionPatch`) that mirror `SysConfig` sections.
Field-level keys like `Piers/WgOn/UpdateMode/...`, runtime values, and auth/session fields exist in both patch structs and persisted config structs, requiring parallel maintenance.
The patch engine (`confPatchField`, parse/apply/merge functions) creates a second schema authority separate from `structs.SysConfig`.
findings.suggestion: Move to section-scoped patching derived from canonical config section types (or generated mapping metadata) so patch schema is declared once per section and reused for parse/apply/merge paths.

findings.summary: The disk adapter layer is largely a pass-through facade over storage, adding indirection without distinct responsibility.
evidence: `system/disk/FstabRecord` duplicates storage record shape and just converts to/from `storage.FstabRecord` for `Line` and `ParseFstabLine`.
Most exported disk functions (`ReadFstabLines`, `WriteFstabLines`, `ListHardDisks`, `CreateGroundSegFilesystem`, etc.) directly call corresponding storage functions with `storage.DefaultSeams()`.
No boundary-specific policy/validation is introduced in this adapter layer.
findings.suggestion: Either collapse callers directly to `system/storage` or define a smaller disk boundary with explicit policy differences (not full API mirroring), then keep only behavior-adding adapters.

findings.summary: Large callback-bag runtimes create mixed responsibilities and force broad test wiring, reducing cohesion of module behavior.
evidence: `Runtime*Ops` in orchestration and `AccessPointRuntime` expose many function fields spanning unrelated concerns (filesystem, network, lifecycle, process probing, config snapshots).
Normalization/defaulting logic has to fill many nil callbacks (`normalizeAccessPointRuntime`, seam bundle builders), indicating object breadth instead of focused collaborators.
Tests introduce extensive callback registration scaffolding (e.g., startup background service callback setter maps and runtime option builders) to control behavior.
findings.suggestion: Split runtime bags into narrower, responsibility-aligned interfaces/components (e.g., process probe, config source, lifecycle executor) and inject only what each flow needs.

findings.summary: Protocol contract/action conformance checks are duplicated across multiple test packages instead of centralized reusable suites.
evidence: Multiple test files separately assert supported action sets, parse behavior, action-contract alignment, and deterministic ordering for overlapping protocol concepts.
`wifi_c2c_flow_test` and `uploadsvc/service_test` each include contract alignment checks that overlap with protocol-level tests, creating duplicated invariant enforcement paths.
When contracts evolve, these checks require synchronized edits in several modules.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared protocol conformance test harness/fixture package and have feature modules consume canonical assertions instead of re-encoding contract invariants locally.

findings.summary: Config naming drifts between `Conf` and `Config`, obscuring a single concept.
evidence: `bootstrap.go` exposes `Conf()` and comments like "global conf var" while newer APIs in the same package use `Config` wording.
`config_events.go` keeps both `StartConfigEventLoop` and legacy aliases `StartConfEventLoop`/`StartConfEventLoopWithRuntime` plus `ConfChannel`.
`config_update_registry.go` uses `confPatch*` type families, reinforcing the abbreviated vocabulary in parallel with non-abbreviated names.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on `Config` terminology for new and internal identifiers (for example `ConfigSnapshot`, `StartConfigEventLoopLegacy` if needed), keep compatibility shims deprecated but clearly marked, and migrate internal type families from `confPatch*` to `configPatch*` in one coordinated rename pass.

findings.summary: Protocol action names use inconsistent token order across layers.
evidence: Contracts define `UploadActionOpenEndpoint` and `UploadActionReset`, but C2C flips shape to `C2CConnectAction`.
Actions package uses another pattern: `ActionUploadOpenEndpoint` and `ActionC2CConnect`.
All map back to shared raw tokens in `actionids.go`, so names differ by layer rather than domain meaning.
findings.suggestion: Adopt one canonical pattern (for example `<Domain><Verb><Object>Action`) and align contracts/actions constants to it, keeping temporary aliases where required for compatibility until references are migrated.

findings.summary: Runtime-injection naming is over-suffixed and inconsistent, reducing intent clarity.
evidence: `state_lifecycle.go` has both `LoadStartramRegionsWithRuntime` and `LoadStartramRegionsWithRuntimeState`, where the second suffix does not add clear semantic distinction.
`config_events.go` multiplies variants (`StartConfigEventLoopWithRuntime`, `StartConfEventLoopWithRuntime`) that differ mostly by compatibility naming.
`operations_runtime.go` repeats near-identical pair naming (`RunTransitionedOperation` and `RunTransitionedOperationWithRuntime`) across many call paths.
findings.suggestion: Define a small, consistent naming matrix for injection variants (for example `X`, `XWithRuntime`, and no additional suffix tiers), collapse ambiguous names like `WithRuntimeState`, and isolate compatibility wrappers under clearly marked legacy names.
